key_insight: High-pressure torsion nanostructures the TiZrHfNbTa refractory alloy so strongly that defect- and grain-boundary scattering suppresses phonon transport more than electron transport, making electrons the dominant carrier of thermal conductivity.
engineering_relevance: Severe plastic deformation can tune thermal and electrical transport in refractory alloys for applications needing extreme-temperature, mechanically robust materials with customized heat dissipation.
method: HPT severe plastic deformation of equiatomic TiZrHfNbTa
Measured thermal/electrical conductivity, specific heat, and hardness versus strain
Applied Wiedemann-Franz analysis to separate electron and phonon contributions
evidence_signal: The abstract reports grain refinement to an average ~40 nm, partial BCC-to-omega transformation, and a phonon share of only about 11–23% of thermal transport across strain levels.
watch_item: The transport trends depend on microstructural state after HPT, so scaling the results to other processing routes or service conditions should be done cautiously.
paper_title: Thermal and electrical conductivity of a refractory high-entropy alloy after high-pressure torsion: Electron versus phonon contributions
